macro_segments.title: The Case of a Minor Girl Married with Father's Knowledge, then Nisuin Without His Consent

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Girl married with father's knowledge, father goes to distant land, she undergoes nisuin without his knowledge
Question: Does nisuin require father's da'at? Can she eat terumah before father protests?
Rav: She presumed to be nesuah, can eat terumah until father protests
Rav Asi: She cannot eat terumah; must fear father will protest
Rav's practical stringency: Agrees with Rav Asi in practice despite theoretical allowance
Rav concedes: If she dies, husband does not inherit (safek nisuin)

macro_segments.title: Contrasting Cases: Kiddushin Without Father's Knowledge, with Father Present and Silent

micro_segments.title: Case: Kiddushin with father's knowledge, but nisuin without his knowledge, father present
Rav Huna: She cannot eat terumah; silence is interpreted as objection
Rav Yirmiah bar Abba: She can eat; silence when father is present means acceptance
Ulla's critique: Huna's position contradictory; Yirmiah's makes more sense

macro_segments.title: The Most Extreme Case: Both Kiddushin and Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge

micro_segments.title: Ulla: Huna's position is intolerable (chometz l'shinayim)
Gemara logic: If kiddushin was good but nisuin questionable = objection, then both questionable = definitely objection

macro_segments.title: The Most Extreme Case: Both Kiddushin and Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava: Reinterprets Huna - two major acts without reaction shows father ceded control
Case: Kiddushin and nisuin both done without father's knowledge

macro_segments.title: Contrasting Cases: Kiddushin Without Father's Knowledge, with Father Present and Silent (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava: Explains Huna - she acts like orphan, father relinquishes control by not objecting to both acts

macro_segments.title: The Halachic Conclusion: Minor Girl's Kiddushin Requires Father or Daughter's Objection

micro_segments.title: Rav: Both father and daughter can object (machot) to kiddushin

macro_segments.title: The Most Extreme Case: Both Kiddushin and Nisuin Without Father's Knowledge (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: She can eat terumah
Rav Yirmiah bar Abba: She cannot eat terumah

macro_segments.title: The Halachic Conclusion: Minor Girl's Kiddushin Requires Father or Daughter's Objection (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rashi: Daughter can only object before father finds out
Tosafot: Clarifies - discussion above only concerns interpreting father's silence
When father explicitly objects or approves, his explicit intent governs the marriage

macro_segments.title: Deriving from Torah: The Case of Seduction and Father's Right to Refuse Marriage

micro_segments.title: Chiya bar Rav: The ketana case is derived from Torah - case of seduction (im ma'en yema'en aviha)
Case interpretation: Does 'only father' in Torah imply daughter can also refuse?
Rav rejects: Seduction in Torah means intercourse not for marriage (lo l'shem ishus)
Torah's intent: If seduction was not for marriage, father's refusal prevents kiddushin and triggers 50 kesef penalty
Gemara reconciliation: Seduction for marriage requires new kiddushin with father's approval (mahor yimharena)
Tosafot's problem: If she can refuse, why would father and husband allow it? When can she actually refuse?
Tosafot's answers: Either father died, or she can object only to force 50 kesef payment even if marriage occurs

macro_segments.title: Marriage with Multiple Items: Which Item Must Be Worth Perutah?

micro_segments.title: Mishna: 'Marry me with this date' repeated multiple times - only works if one date worth perutah
Mishna: 'Marry me with this and this and this' combined - works if combined worth perutah
Mishna: If she eats dates as given, still only works if one date worth perutah by itself
Gemara question: Why does eating matter? Why can't combined dates work even when she's eating?
Interpretation problem: Does mishna's last clause apply to first case (separate statements) or last case (combined)?

macro_segments.title: The Concept of Milveh (Loan): Money That Might Need to Be Returned

micro_segments.title: Rabba and Shmuel: Mishna's clause applies to first case (separate statements)
When she eats dates, might think subjective value makes less than perutah sufficient - clarified that objective value required
Rabbi Ami: Mishna applies to last case (combined) - only last date must be worth perutah
Reasoning: Act of kiddushin not complete until all dates given; earlier dates become milveh (debt) not present goods
Rava infers: Marrying with milveh doesn't work; money that might need return is separate from kiddushin
Inference: Marrying with milveh plus perutah works only if parties focus on concrete perutah
Third inference: Money becomes milveh only if it would need return if kiddushin fails (matana status different)

macro_segments.title: Case: Marriage to Sister - Is the Gift Returned or Kept?

micro_segments.title: Case: Man tries to marry his sister with a ring - marriage obviously void
Rava: Money must be returned (milveh); interpret as bailment (pikadon)
Why not say 'hold for me'? Sister won't agree; this way ring gets into her hand
Shmuel: Money is a gift (matana); can't say 'this is a gift' as it's embarrassing to receive gift
Ravina challenges: If all parties know kiddushin doesn't work, why does it matter if gift or loan?

macro_segments.title: The Hallah Problem: When False Terumah/Hallah Creates Confusion

micro_segments.title: Case: Man separates hallah while still flour (before dough stage) - invalid separation
Question: Can Kohen keep flour if everyone knows it's not valid hallah?
Problem: If Kohen keeps it, he thinks it really is hallah and might rely on it, causing confusion
Gemara: People know rule but not reason - if Kohen keeps flour, might assume it was valid hallah
Solution: Demand return of flour to prevent Kohen from drawing false conclusions about hallah status
Parallel case: Terumah from hydroponics (not biblically obligated) - Kohen keeps but must separate terumah again
Why different? Two vessels involved in hydroponics case; one vessel flour case causes more confusion

topical_tags.term: Torah source - seduction case (im ma'en yema'en aviha)
